era_context: The early 14th century is marked by the fragmentation of Mongol power into regional khanates; the Yuan Dynasty under Yesün Temür controls China amid internal strife; the Mamluk Sultanate dominates the Eastern Mediterranean and Levant; Western Europe is divided among feudal monarchies with the Avignon Papacy exerting spiritual authority; the Mali Empire under Mansa Musa is at its territorial and economic zenith; the Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia; the Aztec Empire is in its early expansion phase in Mesoamerica.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; reliance on later Arab geographies)
Southeast Asia (sparse European documentation; Chinese sources more reliable)
Oceania and Pacific (virtually no written records)
Eastern Europe and the Steppe (limited Western documentation; Russian chronicles underrepresented)
Mesoamerica (limited written records; reliance on later Spanish colonial accounts and archaeological evidence)

events.title: Mansa Musa's hajj to Mecca; Mali's Islamic transformation begins
events.region: Mali Empire, Egypt, Arabia, West Africa
events.description: Mansa Musa, Emperor of the Mali Empire, embarks on his celebrated hajj (pilgrimage) to Mecca. The journey, which takes place over 1324–1325, is one of the most famous pilgrimages in Islamic history. Mansa Musa travels with a massive entourage, including 60,000 people and 80 camels carrying gold. His passage through Egypt and Arabia has profound economic and cultural impacts, introducing Mali to the Islamic world and establishing Mali as a major Islamic power. The hajj marks a turning point in Mali's history, leading to the integration of Islamic scholars, architects, and administrators into Mali's governance.
key_figures: Islamic scholars and administrators encountered during the hajj

sources.name: Ibn Battuta, Rihla (travel account, written c. 1355 but describing 1324–1354 travels)
Al-Umari, Masalik al-absar (written c. 1340, describing Mali c. 1324–1330)

events.certainty_note: Mansa Musa's hajj is attested in contemporary Egyptian records and later Arab geographies. The exact dates (1324–1325) are well-established in Islamic sources.

events.title: Yesün Temür's reign in Yuan Dynasty; internal factional conflicts
events.description: Yesün Temür (also known as Taiding Khan), Khan of the Great Mongols and Emperor of the Yuan Dynasty, continues his reign following his accession in 1323. His rule is marked by struggles between Mongol princes, Chinese bureaucratic interests, and military factions. By 1325, the Yuan Dynasty's internal stability is increasingly fragile, with regional warlords gaining autonomy and the central government's authority weakening. Yesün Temür's attempts to stabilize the dynasty are undermined by these factional conflicts.

sources.name: Yuanshi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty), compiled 14th–15th century
Chinese historiographic consensus on Yuan Dynasty

events.certainty_note: Yesün Temür's reign and the internal conflicts of the Yuan Dynasty are well-attested in official histories, though these were compiled after the dynasty's fall.

events.title: Aztec founding of Tenochtitlan; establishment of the Aztec Empire
events.description: According to Aztec tradition, the Mexica people found the city of Tenochtitlan on an island in Lake Texcoco in 1325. The founding is celebrated in Aztec mythology as the fulfillment of a prophecy: the Mexica were instructed to build their city where they found an eagle perched on a cactus. Tenochtitlan becomes the capital of the Aztec Empire and eventually grows into one of the largest cities in the world. The city's founding marks the beginning of the Aztec Empire's rise to dominance in Mesoamerica.
key_figures: Acamapichtli (first Aztec ruler, r. 1375–1395, but associated with the founding)

sources.name: Codex Mendoza (Aztec pictorial manuscript, compiled 16th century)
Sahagún, Historia general de las cosas de Nueva España (Spanish colonial account, 16th century)
Aztec oral tradition, recorded by Spanish chroniclers

events.certainty_note: The founding of Tenochtitlan in 1325 is based on Aztec tradition and later Spanish colonial accounts. Archaeological evidence suggests the site was occupied earlier, but the traditional date of 1325 is accepted in Aztec historiography. The exact date is uncertain (±5–10 years).

events.title: Muhammad an-Nasir Muhammad's reign in Mamluk Sultanate
events.description: Muhammad an-Nasir Muhammad (r. 1310–1341, with interruptions) continues his reign as Mamluk Sultan of Egypt and the Levant. By 1325, he has consolidated his power and eliminated major rivals. His reign is marked by economic prosperity, the expansion of Cairo, and the strengthening of Islamic institutions. The Mamluk Sultanate under his rule becomes the dominant power in the Eastern Mediterranean and a major patron of Islamic culture.

sources.name: Al-Maqrizi, Kitab al-Suluk (history of the Mamluks, written early 15th century)
Ibn Taghribirdi, al-Nujum al-Zahira (history of the Mamluks, written 15th century)

events.certainty_note: Muhammad an-Nasir Muhammad's reign is attested in later Mamluk chronicles. Contemporary documentation is limited; most sources were compiled centuries later.

events.title: Charles IV of France's reign; feudal consolidation
events.description: Charles IV (r. 1322–1328), the last direct Capetian king of France, continues his reign amid efforts to consolidate royal authority and manage the competing interests of the French nobility. By 1325, Charles IV is aging and has no surviving male heir, setting the stage for the succession crisis that will follow his death in 1328. His reign is marked by complex relations with the Avignon Papacy and feudal conflicts.

sources.name: French royal records and parliamentary rolls

events.certainty_note: Charles IV's reign is well-documented in contemporary French records and later chronicles.
cross_references: 1328-002 (Philip VI's accession following Charles IV's death)

events.title: Osman I's reign in Ottoman Empire; early expansion in Anatolia
events.description: Osman I (Osman Bey), founder of the Ottoman Empire, continues his reign in Anatolia. By 1325, the Ottoman beylik (principality) is expanding at the expense of Byzantine territories. Osman I is consolidating his power and establishing the foundations of what will become the Ottoman Empire. His reign is marked by military campaigns against Byzantine strongholds and the integration of conquered territories into Ottoman administration.
key_figures: Osman I (founder of Ottoman Empire, r. c. 1299–1328)

sources.name: Byzantine sources (Pachymeres, Gregoras), contemporary or near-contemporary

events.certainty_note: Osman I's reign and early Ottoman expansion are attested in Ottoman chronicles (legendary) and Byzantine sources (contemporary but limited). The exact chronology is uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The founding date of Tenochtitlan (1325) is based on Aztec tradition and later Spanish colonial accounts. Archaeological evidence suggests the site was occupied earlier, and the exact date is uncertain (±5–10 years). Modern scholarship emphasizes that the Aztec 'founding' narrative is a mythological construct, not a historical fact. The city's growth was gradual, not sudden. Additionally, the significance of Mansa Musa's hajj is sometimes overstated as a 'turning point' in Mali's history; Mali was already an Islamic power before 1324, and the hajj's impact, while significant, was more gradual than often portrayed. Regarding Yesün Temür's 'consolidation' of power, modern scholarship emphasizes the Yuan Dynasty's structural decline and the growing autonomy of regional warlords, particularly in southern China. The notion of 'stability' under Yesün Temür is therefore misleading.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1325 is uneven across regions. European sources (French royal records, papal documents) are relatively rich and contemporary. Chinese sources (Yuan official histories) are substantial but were compiled by the succeeding Ming Dynasty and reflect Ming biases. Ottoman sources are legendary and were compiled centuries later; Byzantine sources provide some contemporary corroboration but are limited in scope. West African sources are sparse and mediated through later Arab geographies. Mesoamerican sources are limited to later Spanish colonial accounts and Aztec oral tradition recorded by Spanish chroniclers; direct contemporary documentation is absent. The historical record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Court-sponsored narratives dominate; the perspectives of non-elite populations are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: Mansa Musa's hajj occurs during his reign, which began in 1312
Mansa Musa's hajj leads to Mali's Islamic transformation, which Ibn Battuta observes in 1352
Yesün Temür's reign continues from his accession in 1323
Tenochtitlan's founding leads to the Aztec Empire's rise under Acamapichtli and his successors
Tenochtitlan's founding eventually leads to its conquest by Spanish conquistadors in 1519
Muhammad an-Nasir Muhammad's reign continues from his first accession in 1310
Charles IV's reign continues from his accession in 1322
Osman I's reign continues from his accession c. 1299
